section_anchor: Reading Nova Socket against the ladder
brief_summary: Three-panel timeline strip: April 2026's Q1 HE 8K Marble (uniform key grid, no coral highlights, labelled Hall-effect only and committed at manufacture), July 2026's V6 Ultra HE with Nova Socket (a mixed key grid with a handful of coral-filled keys among the outline-only keys, labelled mechanical or TMR chosen per key), and a faded dashed next panel with a question mark standing in for an unannounced future switch technology. A coral bracket spans all three panels with the article's own framing of the hedge.
data_sources: apps/web/src/content/articles/keychron-nova-socket-hybrid.mdx:Reading Nova Socket against the ladder — 'The Q1 HE committed. It's Hall-effect, full stop, and a buyer who wants to run standard mechanical switches on that board can't — the socket geometry doesn't support it.'

apps/web/src/content/articles/keychron-nova-socket-hybrid.mdx:What Nova Socket actually changes — 'That board reads switch position through a Hall-effect sensor under each stem, runs ZMK firmware ... It shipped as the anchor SKU of Keychron's Q Ultra series ... and reached reviewers in April 2026'

apps/web/src/content/articles/keychron-nova-socket-hybrid.mdx:Reading Nova Socket against the ladder — 'Whichever sensing architecture wins — Hall-effect, TMR, or something that hasn't shipped yet — a Nova Socket board can theoretically accommodate it as a socket revision, without asking the buyer to have picked correctly at purchase time.'

apps/web/src/content/articles/keychron-nova-socket-hybrid.mdx:What Nova Socket actually changes — 'A buyer can run all mechanical, all TMR, or mix rows ... without swapping a PCB or buying a second board.'
